---
title: "Spillover of Disease Control Costs In the Presence of Interference"
subtitle: "Modeling Considerations"
format: 
  pdf:
    geometry: "margin=0.75in"
    mathspec: true
    cap-location: bottom
    tbl-cap-location: top
    header-includes: 
      - \usepackage{fancyhdr, amsthm, amssymb,amsfonts,amsthm, amsmath, bbm, tikz}
      - \usepackage{float, tabularx}
      - \usetikzlibrary{positioning, arrows.meta} 
      - \floatplacement{table}{H}
      - \pagestyle{fancy}
      - \fancyhead[R]{Model Considerations}
      - \fancyhead[L]{Trent VanHawkins}
      - \fancyfoot[C]{\thepage} # Center page number at bottom of each page
page-layout: full
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Introduction

Hop Powdery Mildew *Podosphaera macularis* has been the focus of much of the work in this research group. The group has previously investigated spread and control of the disease via Monte-Carlo simulations [@pedro] and has recently taken the first steps in estimating spillover effects of pesticide use using a similar modeling framework (Hwang et. al., 202X). The purpose of this report is to review the modeling structure that has been used up to this point, and to conceptualize this disease pathosystem as a multilayer network and consider modeling implications of this new conceptual framework.

# The Current Modeling Framework

Both of the aforementioned studies have focused on a large dataset of the incidence of disease in hop yards in Oregon to quantify how exposure to powdery mildew causes changes in growers' use of pesticides and the resulting expenditures. Briefly, incidence at yard $i$ was modeled using a GLM framework which accounted for auto-infection and network infection from the previous month, adjusting for the number of fungicide sprays (@eq-old-mod).

$$
\log\left(\frac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right) = \beta + \delta\left(\frac{\tilde{y}_i}{n_{\tilde{y}_i}}\exp(-\eta_1 s_i)\right) + \gamma \sum_{j\neq i} \left(\frac{a_j z_j}{n_{z_j}} \exp(-\eta_2 s_j) w_{ij} f(d_{ij}; \alpha) \right)
$$ {#eq-old-mod}

Inward-directed edge weights for this model are then computed as in @eq-old-edge-weights.

$$
A_{ij} = \begin{cases} 
\gamma_1 \frac{a_j z_j}{n_{z_j}} e^{-\eta_{21} s_j} w_{ij} (1 + D_{ij})^{-\alpha_1} \cdot \mathbb{1}_{V_6}(j) & \text{Yard $i$ is R6} \\
\gamma_2 \frac{a_j z_j}{n_{z_j}} e^{-\eta_{22} s_j} w_{ij} (1 + D_{ij})^{-\alpha_2} & \text{Yard $i$ is non-R6}
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-old-edge-weights}

While this model is certainly a powerful and effective framework, it has some notable limitations. Primarily, estimating these parameters for different types of nodes—such as resistant versus non-resistant cultivars—currently requires fitting a separate model for each node type. This makes it difficult to account for the dynamic interactions and spillover effects between different classes of yards. We now posit the idea that auto-infection and dispersal dynamics do not differ between yard types and that leveraging all data within a single, unified framework will improve statistical power and reduce bias in prameter estimates. 

::: {#tbl-model-specs}
\begin{table}
    \small
    \centering
    \subcaptionbox{Covariate notations and descriptions.\label{tbl-covariates}}{
        \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{lX}
            \toprule
            Covariate & Description \\
            \midrule
            $\tilde{y}_i$     & Number of diseased plants at yard $i$ in the prior month \\
            $n_{\tilde{y}_i}$ & Number of plants sampled at yard $i$ in the prior month \\
            $a_i$             & Area (hectares) of yard $i$ \\
            $z_j$             & Number of diseased plants at yard $j$ in the prior month \\
            $n_{z_j}$         & Number of plants sampled at yard $j$ in the prior month \\
            $a_j$             & Area (hectares) of yard $j$ \\
            $D_{ij}$          & Distance (kilometers) from centroids of yard $i$ to yard $j$ \\
            $w_{ij}$          & Wind vector on $j - i$ direction in the prior month \\
            $s_i$             & Number of fungicide sprays in yard $i$ in prior month \\
            $t_j$             & Number of fungicide sprays in yard $j$ in prior month \\
            \bottomrule
        \end{tabularx}
    }
    
    \vspace{1em}  

    \subcaptionbox{Model parameters and descriptions.\label{tbl-parameters}}{
        \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{lX}
            \toprule
            Parameter & Description \\
            \midrule
            $\beta$    & Baseline log-odds of disease, after accounting for autoinfection and dispersal. \\
            $\delta$   & Change in log-odds of disease associated with autoinfection at the yard scale, after accounting for disease spread. \\
            $\gamma$   & Distance-adjusted change in log-odds of disease associated with disease spread from other yards, after accounting for autoinfection. \\
            $\alpha$   & Dispersal parameter providing distance adjustment to change in log-odds of disease associated with individual source yards. \\
            $\eta_1$   & Change in log-odds of disease associated with autoinfection at the yard scale, after accounting for fungicide sprays. \\
            $\eta_2$   & Dispersal parameter providing fungicide spray adjustment to change in log-odds of disease associated with individual source yards. \\
            \bottomrule
        \end{tabularx}
    }
\end{table}

\small Descriptions of model parameters and covariates notations for each yard of interest $I = 1,2,...,N$ in a month, and all other yards $j = 1,2,.., M_i$ relative to yard $i$.
:::

# Constraining Network Asymmetry

Our first goal is to modify our model such that we can account for the dispersal dynamics among and between different node types. The dataset provides information of whether the cultivar is of type R6 or non-R6 and initial strain of *P. macularis* detected at each yard (V6 or non-V6), where V6 strains have adapted to overcome R6-resistance. Thus, V6 strains of the disease can infect any yard while non-V6 strains can only infect non-R6 yards. Conditioning on the cultivar and initial strain of an initial yard, we con construct a simplified graph of possible network transmission (@fig-transmission-graph).

::: {#fig-transmission-graph}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    node style/.style={circle, draw=black, minimum size=22mm, align=center, font=\small\bfseries, thick},
    edge style/.style={thick, >=Stealth},
    v6_style/.style={fill=orange!20},
    non_v6_style/.style={fill=blue!10}
]

    \node[node style, v6_style]     (R6)   at (0, 0)   {R6};
    \node[node style, non_v6_style] (NR6)  at (4, 0)   {Non-R6};

    % V6 strain: bidirectional arrow between R6 and Non-R6
    \draw[edge style, <->, orange!70!black]
        (R6) to[bend left=0]
        node[midway, above, font=\scriptsize, orange!70!black] {V6}
        (NR6);

    % Non-V6 strain: self-loop on Non-R6
    \draw[edge style, ->, blue!60!black]
        (NR6) to[out=330, in=30, looseness=3]
        node[right, font=\scriptsize, blue!60!black] {Non-V6}
        (NR6);

    % V6 strain: self-loop on R6
    \draw[edge style, ->, orange!70!black]
        (R6) to[out=210, in=150, looseness=3]
        node[left, font=\scriptsize, orange!70!black] {V6}
        (R6);

    % Legend
    \begin{scope}[shift={(8, 0)}]
        \draw[black, thin, dashed] (-0.3, 1.2) rectangle (2.8, -0.3);
        \node[anchor=west, font=\small\itshape] at (-0.2, 0.9) {Initial strain:};
        \draw[orange!70!black, thick, <->] (0, 0.45) -- (0.6, 0.45);
        \node[anchor=west, font=\small] at (0.7, 0.45) {V6};
        \draw[blue!60!black, thick, ->]   (0, 0.05) -- (0.6, 0.05);
        \node[anchor=west, font=\small] at (0.7, 0.05) {Non-V6};
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}

\small A simplified graph of possible network transmission between yards, conditioning on
cultivar type and initial strain. R6 and non-R6 yards transmit to each other under a V6
initial strain, while non-R6 yards under a non-V6 strain can only infect other non-R6 yards
(self-loop).
:::

Now, let us leverage this known relationship to construct an adjacency matrix $(\mathbf{A})$ which encodes possible transmission. First let us define

$$
c_k = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{cultivar} = \text{R6} \\
0 & \text{cultivar} = \text{non-R6}
\end{cases} \qquad s_k = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{strain} = \text{V6} \\
0 & \text{strain} = \text{non-V6}
\end{cases}
$$

The entries of $\mathbf{A}$ are then given by

$$
\mathbf{A}_{ij} = \begin{cases}
1 & s_j = 1 \text{ or } (c_i = c_j = 0) \\
0 & \text{else}
\end{cases}
$$

Suppose we order yards $i = 1, \ldots, N$ by cultivar type (non-R6 then R6). We can conceptualize $\mathbf{A}$ as the following block-matrix:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{A}_{\text{NR6} \to \text{NR6}} & \mathbf{A}_{\text{R6} \to \text{NR6}} \\
\mathbf{A}_{\text{NR6} \to \text{R6}} & \mathbf{A}_{\text{R6} \to \text{R6}}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Note that the entries of the upper-triangular blocks are all 1, however the entries of $\mathbf{A}_{\text{NR6} \to \text{R6}}$ can be 0 if the initial strain is not V6. Leveraging this known network structure, we can modify our model to estimate the different types of dispersal events. Preserving notation from the previous model, let us generalize our expression for the auto-infection and dispersal kernels. Define $\mathcal{X} = \{\tilde{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{n}_{\tilde{y}}, \mathbf{t}\}, \mathcal{Y} = \{\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{z}, \mathbf{n}_{z_j}, \mathbf{d}, \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{t}\}$

$$
A(\mathcal{X}; \eta_1) = \left(\frac{\tilde{y}_i}{n_{\tilde{y}_i}}T_1(\mathbf{t}; \eta_1)\right) \quad  D(\mathcal{Y}; \eta_2, \alpha) =  \frac{a_j z_j}{n_{z_j}} \cdot  T_2(\mathbf{t}; \eta_2) \cdot  \mathbf{w} \cdot f(\mathbf{d}; \alpha)
$$ 

We could then define a new model as in @eq-new-mod.

$$
\begin{split}
\log\left(\frac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right) = \beta + \delta A(\mathcal{X}; \eta) &+ \gamma \sum_{j \neq i} \left(\mathbf{A}_{ij} \cdot D(\mathcal{Y}; \eta_2, \alpha) \right)
\end{split}
$$ {#eq-new-mod}

We can then construct inward edge-weights for all nodes using the dispersal component as in @eq-edge-weight.
$$
W_{ij} = \gamma \cdot \mathbf{A}_{ij} \cdot D(\mathcal{X}; \eta_2, \alpha)  
$${#eq-edge-weight}

In the particular case of the previous model formulation, our model will be formulated as 

$$
\log\left(\frac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right) = \beta + \delta\left(\frac{\tilde{y}_i}{n_{\tilde{y}_i}}\exp(-\eta_1 s_i)\right) + \gamma \sum_{j\neq i} \left(\mathbf{A}_{ij} \frac{a_j z_j}{n_{z_j}} \exp(-\eta_2 s_j) w_{ij} f(d_{ij}; \alpha) \right)
$$
